1.pericope_label: Greeting: A Prisoner, Not an Apostle
1.original_language: Paul's self-title is desmios (“prisoner”), chosen over apostolos — an appeal to sympathy, not office. Philemon is agapetos (“beloved”) and synergos (“fellow-worker”), a partner in the gospel labor rather than a subordinate.
1.historical_context: The opening follows the standard Greco-Roman letter form (sender, recipients, greeting), but Paul widens it to the household and “the church in your house” (v.2). First-century congregations met in homes like Philemon's, so the whole community would hear this appeal read aloud.
1.christ: Grace and peace come jointly “from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ” (v.3) — Father and Son named as one source, an unforced early witness to Christ's deity, and the note of grace under which the whole letter unfolds.
1.exposition: Every word of the greeting is chosen to disarm. Paul, who could write as an apostle with authority to command, instead calls himself a prisoner of Christ Jesus — appealing to sympathy, not office. He names Philemon “beloved” and “fellow-worker,” and widens the address to the whole household and the church that meets in the home, so that the coming plea for a runaway slave will be heard within the affection and witness of the Christian community. Then the apostolic blessing of grace and peace hallows even this domestic errand. Before a single request is made, the ground has been prepared: this will be a letter of love and entreaty, not of command.

verses.note: Paul names himself not apostle but a prisoner of Christ Jesus — and the choice is deliberate. eastern Chrysostom sees it strike Philemon with compunction, quenching anger before a word of the case is spoken; grammatical-historical Robertson pictures Paul writing with his manacled hand, the chains themselves a silent argument. Philemon is greeted as “beloved” and a “fellow-worker” — a partner, not a subordinate to be commanded.

The address widens to Apphia, Archippus, and the church in your house. Apphia is generally taken as Philemon’s wife, closely concerned in a domestic matter; Archippus a “fellow-soldier,” perhaps his son and a minister (Colossians 4:17). Though the appeal is personal, Paul lays it before the household and congregation — a gentle publicity that surrounds the master with the community’s witness as he is asked to forgive.

Grace to you and peace — the same benediction Paul gives his weightier letters. grammatical-historical Gill notes the little epistle is framed like the great ones, inscription and salutation complete; the smallness of the matter does not lessen the blessing. Grace and peace come jointly “from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ,” Father and Son named as one source.

witnesses.point: Calling himself a prisoner, Paul “strikes Philemon with compunction,” quenching anger from the very first line.
“Prisoner” (desmios) stands in place of “apostle” as the more touching word — Paul writing with his manacled right hand.
Admires the loftiness of mind Paul shows in handling so lowly a subject as a runaway slave.

external.name: Scot McKnight
external.point: The greeting to “the church in your house” frames the whole letter as a matter for the worshiping community, not a private transaction.

1.application: Notice how Paul begins where he means to end — in grace. The hardest asks are best carried on the warmest words.

4.original_language: Koinonia (“fellowship/partnership”) in v.6 is the letter's hinge-word — the active sharing of faith Paul prays will become energes (“effective”). Splanchna (v.7, “hearts,” literally the inward parts) recurs three times as the seat of deep affection.
4.christ: Philemon's love and faith are directed “toward the Lord Jesus” (v.5); the “refreshment” his love gives echoes the rest Christ promises (Matthew 11:28) — the very word Paul will ask Philemon to extend to him in v.20.
4.exposition: Paul does not rush to his request. First he thanks God for Philemon and prays for him constantly, grounding his gratitude in the report — likely from Epaphras, perhaps from Onesimus himself — of Philemon’s love and faith toward the Lord Jesus and all the saints. He prays that the sharing of that faith may grow effective in the full knowledge of every good thing in them (v.6), and rejoices that Philemon’s love has refreshed the hearts of the saints. The praise is sincere, not flattery — yet it is also strategy: a man whose love has refreshed so many will surely refresh one more.

verses.note: Before any request, Paul thanks God and prays for Philemon always. eastern Chrysostom marks the method: he does not ask the favor at once, but first praises and shows his love, winning the heart before the appeal. reformed Calvin adds a fine point — Paul prays for the very grace he gives thanks for, since grace already given still needs increase.

The thanksgiving rests on what Paul has heard: Philemon’s love and faith. Calvin observes these two together comprise “the whole perfection of a Christian” — faith looking to Christ, love reaching to the neighbor. The expositors note the clause is commonly distributed: faith toward the Lord Jesus, love toward all the saints.

Paul’s prayer proper: that the fellowship of Philemon’s faith may become effective through the full knowledge of every good thing in him. reformed Calvin clarifies this is not more praise but a blessing asked; grammatical-historical Robertson reads koinonia as the active partnership of faith, at work in love — precisely the love Paul is about to request.

Paul’s joy: Philemon’s love has refreshed the hearts of the saints. grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the verb for “refresh” is Christ’s own word for the rest he gives (Matthew 11:28). The tender “brother” marks Paul an equal — and quietly plants a word he will reclaim in v.20: “refresh my heart.”

witnesses.point: Paul praises before he petitions — “having shown his love,” he wins the heart before he asks.
Love and faith together are “the whole perfection of a Christian man”; Paul prays for the increase of the grace he already gives thanks for.
“Refresh” (anapauo) echoes Christ’s promise of rest — a word Paul will ask Philemon to fulfill toward himself.

external.name: N.T. Wright
external.point: The thanksgiving already sounds the letter’s key word, koinonia — partnership — which becomes the hinge of the appeal in v.17.

4.application: Genuine praise and real request are not opposites. Paul honors Philemon truthfully, and that truth becomes the ground on which he can ask something costly.

8.original_language: Paul could speak with parresia (“boldness”) but chooses parakaleo (“I appeal”). He calls himself presbytes — “aged,” though the word can also mean “ambassador,” a fitting double sense — and plays on the name Onesimus (from onesis, “profit”): once achrestos (“useless”), now euchrestos (“useful”).
8.historical_context: Under Roman law a runaway slave (fugitivus) could be branded, flogged, or worse, and whoever harbored one was liable to the owner. Paul, himself in chains, sends Onesimus back into that legal jeopardy — the appeal is made against a real and dangerous social backdrop.
8.christ: Paul calls Onesimus “my child, whom I have begotten in my chains” (v.10) — spiritual sonship created in Christ, the new-birth relationship that now reframes a runaway slave as a beloved son.
8.exposition: Now the plea begins — and its first move is renunciation. Though Paul could boldly command what is fitting, for love’s sake he would rather beseech (v.9) — as “Paul the aged,” and now a prisoner. Only when the heart is fully softened does he name the one for whom he pleads: Onesimus, my child, whom I have begotten in my chains. And he wraps the runaway in a gentle pun — once useless, the slave whose name means “useful” is now useful indeed, to Philemon and to Paul alike.

verses.note: Paul opens the appeal by laying his authority down: though he might command what is fitting, he will not. grammatical-historical Barnes grants his apostolic right to enjoin; puritan Matthew Henry marks that the main business now begins. The refusal to command is itself the argument — a freely granted favor is worth more than an extorted one.

“For love’s sake I rather appeal” — and two words disarm: Paul the aged, and now also a prisoner. reformed Calvin notes he chooses entreaty over command out of love; Barnes, that an old man is an object of veneration. Who could refuse an aged, imprisoned apostle who asks, in love, rather than commands?

At last the name, held back until the heart is won: my child Onesimus, begotten in my chains. grammatical-historical Ellicott admires the tact — the name withheld until Philemon’s interest is “doubly engaged.” Paul gives the runaway a title he grants elsewhere only to Timothy and Titus: not “your slave” but “my son,” converted under Paul’s prison ministry.

A graceful play on the name: Onesimus means “useful,” yet he had been useless — the runaway who wronged his master — and now is useful to both. Robertson notes the pun (onesis, profit; achrestos, useless); Ellicott, that the Greek fathers oddly missed it. Grace has made the useless useful, the loss a gain.

witnesses.point: After so many praises and so much preparation, only now does Paul “introduce this great matter.”
The name “Onesimus” is withheld to the last, after Philemon’s interest has been doubly engaged.
Marks the depth of Paul’s condescension — owning a despised runaway slave as his own son.

external.name: John M.G. Barclay
external.point: Paul’s refusal to command, while still applying every ounce of relational pressure, is the letter’s central and much-debated rhetorical strategy.

8.application: Authority laid down can move a heart that authority pressed would only harden. Paul asks the way love asks.

12.pericope_label: No Longer a Slave, but a Brother
12.original_language: Paul “sent him back” (anepempsa, an epistolary aorist — “I am herewith returning him”), calling Onesimus ta ema splanchna, “my very heart.” In v.16 the slave is “no longer as a doulos” — still legally a slave, yet now something that overflows the category.
12.historical_context: Manumission (the formal freeing of a slave) was common in the Roman world but entirely the owner's prerogative. Paul never names it; he reframes the relationship so completely that, as many have seen, the institution cannot finally survive the acknowledgment of a slave as brother.
12.christ: “A beloved brother… both in the flesh and in the Lord” (v.16) applies the one-new-humanity of Galatians 3:28 to a single household: in Christ the bond of brotherhood outranks the bond of ownership.
12.exposition: Paul sends Onesimus back — the gospel does not cancel a lawful debt — yet sends him as his own heart. He confesses he would gladly have kept the useful convert to serve him in Philemon’s place (v.13), but would do nothing without Philemon’s consent, that the kindness be willing, not forced. Then he lifts the whole case into providence and grace: perhaps Onesimus was parted for a while that Philemon might have him back forever — no longer as a slave, but as a beloved brother. Here the letter turns: the runaway returns not merely restored but transformed.

verses.note: Paul returns the converted slave — grammatical-historical Adam Clarke notes that “the Christian religion never cancels any civil relations” — yet calls him his very heart. reformed Calvin sees the sharpest argument here: to refuse Onesimus would be to wound Paul’s own heart.

Paul discloses how he valued the man: he would gladly have kept him to serve in Philemon’s stead during his imprisonment. The slave Philemon counted worthless, Paul found so useful he longed to keep him — a quiet reminder, too, that Philemon (Paul’s own convert) owed the apostle service.

But Paul would do nothing without Philemon’s consent, that the good be voluntary, not forced. reformed Calvin grounds this in the rule that no sacrifice pleases God but the freely offered one (2 Corinthians 9:7). The respect is itself persuasive: trusted and left free, Philemon is the more moved to give.

Paul reads providence gently: “perhaps he was parted from you for a while” — the soft verb veiling the flight — “that you might have him back forever.” reformed Calvin: God turns evils done in malice to a good end. puritan Matthew Henry: such changed characters become a blessing.

The turn of the whole letter: Onesimus returns no longer as a slave but above a slave — a beloved brother, dear “both in the flesh and in the Lord.” grammatical-historical Ellicott discerns here a principle “absolutely destructive” of slavery: the brotherhood of master and slave in Christ must, in time, undo the institution itself. Paul commands no emancipation — but plants the seed that makes it inevitable.

witnesses.point: Justice required the return: “the Christian religion never cancels any civil relations” — yet grace transforms them.
To refuse Onesimus would be cruelty against Paul’s own heart, whom Philemon so loves.
“No longer as a slave” states a principle absolutely destructive of slavery — the deeper equality effacing the outward.

external.name: F.F. Bruce
external.point: Paul never issues a call for abolition, yet the letter breathes “an atmosphere in which the institution could only wilt and die.”

external.name: N.T. Wright
external.point: “Both in the flesh and in the Lord” reframes the master–slave bond as a family relationship the gospel has already remade.

12.application: The gospel rarely abolishes a wrong by decree; it dissolves it by making the wronged and the wrongdoer brothers. Ask where a relationship you hold as “transaction” is really a brotherhood waiting to be owned.

17.pericope_label: Receive Him as Myself — Charge It to Me
17.original_language: Koinonos (“partner,” v.17) is the personal form of the koinonia of v.6 — the appeal turns on their partnership. “Charge it to my account” is ellogao, a commercial term for entering a debt in a ledger; “I will repay” (apotino, v.19) is the language of a formal IOU; and “let me benefit” (onaimen, v.20) puns once more on Onesimus's name.
17.historical_context: Verse 19 is a legally recognizable bond: “I, Paul, write this with my own hand” marks a cheirograph, a handwritten promissory note. Paul turns a private letter into a binding financial guarantee, assuming a debtor-slave's liability himself.
17.christ: Here the letter's gospel shape is clearest: the one who owes nothing takes on the debtor's debt so the wronged party may receive him freely — a transparent image of Christ made our surety, bidding the Father charge our account to him. Philemon's own unpayable debt (“you owe me your very self,” v.19) mirrors the believer's debt of grace.
17.exposition: Now the climax. On the ground of their partnership — if you consider me a partner, receive him as you would receive me — Paul identifies himself wholly with the runaway. Then, in his own hand, he assumes the debt: if he has wronged you or owes you anything, charge that to my account; I, Paul, will repay it — with a whispered reminder that Philemon owes Paul his very self. The witnesses have long seen the shape of the gospel here: the one with no debt standing surety for the debtor. And Paul closes the plea with a last pun on the name — let me have benefit of you; refresh my heart — asking for himself the very refreshment Philemon has given so many.

verses.note: The summit: receive him as myself. eastern Chrysostom marks that only after all the preparation does Paul “introduce this great matter”; grammatical-historical Robertson calls it “surpassing delicacy and consummate tact.” On the bond of partnership (koinonos), to refuse Onesimus would now be to refuse Paul himself.

“If he has wronged you or owes you anything, charge it to me.” grammatical-historical Robertson notes the runaway had “probably robbed Philemon before he ran away”; reformed Calvin infers the same. Paul makes himself surety — and many have seen a faint image of the gospel: the one with no debt bearing the debt of the one who owes.

Paul signs the bond in his own hand — “I, Paul, will repay it” — then, with delicate irony, “not to mention that you owe me your very self.” grammatical-historical Ellicott calls it “a regular bond couched in legal form”; the reminder is unanswerable — how refuse a small debt to one who owes Paul his own soul?

“Yes, brother, let me have benefit of you” — Robertson hears the pun on Onesimus (“profitable”) — “refresh my heart in Christ.” Paul reclaims the word of v.7: the man who refreshed so many saints is now asked to refresh the apostle, by receiving one more.

witnesses.point: “Receive him as myself” — a request of “surpassing delicacy and consummate tact.”
Paul puts the runaway in his own room, and is so confident of Philemon that he could ask anything.
“I will repay” is a regular bond in legal form — Paul’s signed note of hand.

external.name: Markus Barth & Helmut Blanke
external.point: The “charge it to my account” is read as the letter’s theological center — Paul’s surety mirroring the logic of atonement.

external.name: N.T. Wright
external.point: The appeal turns on koinonia: because Paul and Philemon are partners, Onesimus is received into the same partnership.

17.application: “Charge it to my account” is the gospel in four words. Someone stood surety for your debt; here Paul asks Philemon to let that same grace flow one step further, to Onesimus.

21.historical_context: “Do even more than I say” (v.21) is widely read as a veiled hint at manumission — Paul leaves the costly, public act of freeing Onesimus to Philemon's own initiative. The request to “prepare a guest room” (xenia) assumes the hospitality networks that carried traveling apostles between house churches.
21.christ: Paul expects release “through your prayers” (v.22): the intercession of the church and the providence of God in Christ frame even his travel plans, and the promised visit becomes a gentle accountability for how Onesimus is received.
21.exposition: Paul closes the appeal not in anxiety but in confidence — sure of Philemon’s obedience, and knowing you will do even more than I say. The expositors hear in that “more” an unspoken hope of manumission: Paul hints at freedom for Onesimus without ever commanding it. Then a last, quietly persuasive request: prepare a guest room for me — Paul expects, through their prayers, to be released and to come. The prospect of his arrival is a gentle accountability: Philemon will soon host the apostle, and Paul will see how the runaway was received.

verses.note: Paul writes confident of Philemon’s obedience, “knowing you will do even more than I say.” grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: “some think the apostle hints that he should manumit Onesimus”; Robertson agrees Paul “knows” Philemon will free the slave, but prefers it come freely. The trust itself binds Philemon to fulfill it.

“Prepare a lodging for me, for I trust that through your prayers I shall be granted to you.” reformed Calvin notes this confidence “powerfully moved Philemon”: the apostle would soon come and see how Onesimus fared — a tender accountability — and Paul asks not grand quarters but a simple guest room.

witnesses.point: “Do more than I say” is widely taken as a delicate hint that Philemon should set Onesimus free.
The promise of a visit powerfully moved Philemon — he would soon answer to Paul in person for Onesimus’s welcome.

external.name: John M.G. Barclay
external.point: The “more than I say” is exactly where the letter’s ambiguity peaks — freedom is implied, never demanded, leaving the decision Philemon’s own.

21.application: Trust can be a gift that shapes the one trusted. Paul expects the best of Philemon — and expecting it helps make it so.

23.historical_context: The greeters — Epaphras, Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, Luke — are the same circle named in Colossians 4:10–14, confirming Philemon lived at Colossae and dating the letter to Paul's Roman imprisonment. Epaphras was the Colossians' own founding minister (Colossians 1:7).
23.christ: “The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit” (v.25) closes the letter as it opened (v.3) — an inclusio of grace. The plural “your” sets the whole household, Onesimus now among them, under the grace of Christ that reconciles.
23.exposition: The letter ends as it began — surrounded by the fellowship. Epaphras, Philemon’s own Colossian pastor and Paul’s fellow-prisoner, sends greeting, and with him Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke — the same circle named in Colossians, their united affection lending weight to the appeal. Then the benediction: the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit — plural, falling on the whole house. The letter that opened in grace closes in grace, leaving the case of Onesimus in the hands of the grace that reconciles.

verses.note: Epaphras, Paul’s “fellow-prisoner,” sends greeting. grammatical-historical Robertson identifies him as the Colossian preacher who came to Rome to enlist Paul against the rising error of the Lycus valley (Colossians 4:12). Philemon’s own pastor joins the appeal — his townsman’s concurrence surrounding the master.

Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke — the co-workers of Colossians 4:10–14. reformed Calvin notes soberly that Demas, here honored a fellow-worker, would later forsake Paul (2 Timothy 4:10) — a warning against presuming on a single season of zeal.

“The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit.” puritan Matthew Henry: grace is the best of wishes, with which Paul begins and ends. The “your” is plural — the blessing falls on Apphia, Archippus, and the whole church, sealing with “Amen” a letter that has been, throughout, a model of grace.

witnesses.point: Epaphras is the Colossian preacher who came to Rome to enlist Paul against the rising error in the Lycus valley.
Demas, here a fellow-worker, would afterward forsake Paul — a warning against presumption.
Grace is the best wish, with which Paul both begins and ends; the plural “your” blesses the whole household.

external.name: Scot McKnight
external.point: The named greeters are the same house-church network of Colossians — the community that will actually witness Onesimus received as a brother at the table.

23.application: The appeal ends where it must: not in a rule enforced but in grace invoked over a whole household — the only atmosphere in which a slave can become a brother.

reflection: Paul refuses to command and instead appeals “for love's sake” (v.9). Where do you reach for authority when love would move a person further?
Onesimus returns “no longer as a slave but as a beloved brother” (v.16). Whom do you relate to as a category or a grievance, when Christ would have you receive them as family?
“Charge it to my account; I will repay” (vv.18–19) pictures the gospel. Where is God asking you to absorb a cost so that someone else can be reconciled?
The letter opens and closes in grace (v.3, v.25) over a whole household. What relationship in your community needs that atmosphere of grace?
